core_concepts: Definition of Reps and Sets: A rep is a single execution of an exercise; a set is a grouping of repetitions performed consecutively without rest.
Training Volume: Muscle growth is primarily a volume-driven adaptation. 10 sets per muscle group per week is established as a baseline threshold for effective hypertrophy.
Frequency: Spreading training volume across multiple sessions per week (2-3x/week) is generally more effective for muscle protein synthesis than infrequent, massive 'body-part' sessions.

Hypertrophy Basics: Strength (low reps, high load) and hypertrophy (moderate reps, moderate load) exist on a spectrum, but 8-12 reps per set is the classic benchmark for hypertrophy due to the balance of mechanical tension and metabolic stress.
Progressive Overload: The necessity of consistently increasing the weight, reps, or sets over time to force the body to adapt to new stress levels.

actionable_advice.sets_and_reps: Beginner standard: 3 sets of 8–12 repetitions. Focus on hitting failure by the final set.
actionable_advice.frequency: Aim for 2–3 training days per week initially to allow for adequate recovery. As fitness increases, frequency can scale up to 4+ days.
actionable_advice.intensity: Adjust weights so that the prescribed rep range is challenging but doable with perfect form; increase weight once the top of the rep range is consistently reached.
actionable_advice.progression_methods: Track your lifts in a logbook. Aim for small, incremental increases in weight or reps every 1-2 weeks.
actionable_advice.recovery_advice: Listen to your body, prioritize rest days, and ensure a balanced approach to full-body movements rather than over-specializing early on.
actionable_advice.exercise_selection: Prioritize core compound lifts (squat, deadlift, press patterns) for maximum movement efficiency and foundational strength.

scientific_claims.claim: Muscle growth is largely volume-dependent, with ~10 sets/week per muscle being a highly effective target.
Higher frequency (training muscles 2-3x/week) is superior to 1x/week for natural lifters.
8-12 reps is an optimal 'standard' range for hypertrophy due to its efficiency in balancing metabolic stress and load.

coaching_insights: Avoid the 'bro-split' trap; beginners benefit more from frequent, lower-volume exposures across the whole body.
Logbooks are not optional; they are the most important tool for ensuring objective progressive overload.
Discomfort is not synonymous with progress; ensure the client learns to distinguish between muscle fatigue and joint pain.
common_mistakes: Switching routines too frequently ('program hopping') instead of allowing time for adaptation.
Ignoring the rest periods between sets, which are critical for maintaining intensity.
Ego lifting: Prioritizing weight over range of motion, which limits muscle stimulus.
Neglecting compound movements in favor of isolation exercises.
Inconsistent gym attendance or failing to track performance metrics.

myths_addressed: Myth: You need to be sore to make progress.
Myth: Beginners should train 6 days a week.
Myth: Lifting heavy is bad for joints.
Myth: You can 'tone' muscle without progressive overload.
Myth: Supplements are more important than your routine.
Myth: Isolation exercises build more muscle than compounds.
Myth: You should train to absolute failure every set.
Myth: Women will get bulky from lifting weights.
Myth: You need to do 20+ exercises per session.

grit_application.goal: Embed tracking and foundational terminology into the user's daily habit.
grit_application.routine: Create a 'Fundamentals Log' that requires users to track at least 3 compound movements per session to ensure objective progression.
grit_application.instruction: Add tooltips in the UI that define 'Sets' and 'Reps' at the point of entry so beginners never feel intimidated by gym terminology.
grit_application.tracking: Enable a 'Volume Heatmap' that visually shows which muscle groups the user has targeted over the last 7 days, ensuring they are hitting the 10-set-per-muscle baseline.
